_provenance.discipline: Process-not-people; primary-source-anchored. Three structural corrections applied: (1) headline rewritten to fix the 5-vs-40 cardinal sin both Gemini and ChatGPT flagged; (2) constitutional framing rebuilt around Johor's own State Legal Adviser written opinion of 11 Sept 2025, which calls appointed ADUN inconsistent with the Federal Constitution — the strongest primary source available, and one the original Stage 1 missed; (3) gender dimension added via the 9-NGO Projek SAMA intervention on the same day. Election deadline corrected from March 2027 to mid-2027 (5-year term anchored to first sitting 21 Apr 2022, election within 60 days of dissolution per Eighth Schedule §9(3)-(4)).

revision_log: CORRECTED (Stage 3): INC#1 '5 = 40 seats BN won' math error fixed in hook (per-card revisions document the wording change)

CORRECTED (Stage 3): INC#2 'March 2027 constitutional deadline' dropped from view card; the 5-year term runs from first sitting 21 Apr 2022 so dissolution is in April 2027 with election within 60 days. View card softened to 'before voters next vote' rather than asserting a wrong specific date.
REMOVED (Stage 3): INC#3 'Onn Hafiz 5-year term from MB swearing-in 15 Mar 2022' framing dropped — assembly term runs from first sitting, not MB oath
CORRECTED (Stage 3): MIS#1 'does not, on its face, ban additional appointed members' replaced with §4(1) ordinary-state text reading per per-card revision tag on card[1]

CORRECTED (Stage 3): MIS#2 'Pahang ran the same playbook' replaced with specific chronology contrast — Pahang took four years between enacting (Nov 2020) and appointing (Sep 2024); Johor compressed both into one day
CORRECTED (Stage 3): MIS#3 'party officials, not technocrats' replaced with 'party labels' and Pahang's actual rationale 'persons of calibre' per per-card revision tag on card[2]
REMOVED (Stage 3): MIS#4 'none were non-aligned experts' — absorbed into MIS#3 fix; cards no longer assert anything about expert qualifications

REMOVED (Stage 3): MIS#5 '30-day Sultan assent rule' — dropped from cards; the procedural detail not load-bearing for the editorial argument and the strict deemed-publication caveat would have over-complicated

REMOVED (Stage 3): MIS#6 'Sabah 6 since Dec 2025' — Sabah/Terengganu state-by-state comparison dropped from cards because Stage 3 flagged dating imprecision and the structural claim does not require it; Sarawak counter-example in view card carries the contrast
REMOVED (Stage 3): MIS#7 'Pahang only filled-out precedent' — claim contradicted by Sabah/Terengganu records; dropped from cards
headline: Johor Voted 40-16 To Add Five Appointed Assemblymen — Eight Months After Its Own Legal Adviser Said No
context: On 7 May 2026, the Johor State Legislative Assembly voted 40-16 to amend the Undang-undang Tubuh Negeri Johor 1895, allowing up to five appointed members to join the 56 elected ones. The split tracked the 2022 state-election seat count exactly. Eight months earlier, on 11 September 2025, Johor's own State Legal Adviser had stated in a written assembly answer that appointed assemblymen were inconsistent with the Federal Constitution.

cards.big: Johor's state assembly voted 40-16 to add up to five appointed members. The split was the 2022 election, replayed on the floor.
cards.sub: Barisan Nasional's 40-seat majority from 2022 cleared the two-thirds amendment bar. Eight months earlier, Johor's own State Legal Adviser had said this was inconsistent with the Federal Constitution.
sources: Malay Mail, 7 May 2026 — vote and rationale: https://www.malaymail.com/news/malaysia/2026/05/07/johor-mb-says-inclusion-of-five-non-elected-assembly-seats-to-widen-policy-input/219037
The Vibes, 7 May 2026 — '40 in favour, 16 against': https://www.thevibes.com/index.php/articles/news/122699/johor-assembly-passes-bill-allowing-appointment-of-non-elected-assemblymen
Election Commission of Malaysia (SPR) official 2022 Johor results — BN 40 of 56: https://mysprsemak.spr.gov.my/semakan/keputusan/pru-dun

Official Johor Assembly written answer of 11 September 2025 — State Legal Adviser viewed appointed ADUN as inconsistent with the Federal Constitution: https://dewannegeri.johor.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/SOALAN-LISAN-YANG-TIDAK-HABIS-DIJAWAB-PADA-11-SEPT-2025.pdf
cards.revisions: CORRECTED (Stage 3): old headline 'expanded by exactly the seats BN won in 2022' → fixed; 5 ≠ 40 was a cardinal-sin mathematical error flagged by both Gemini and ChatGPT. INTRODUCED (Stage 6 self-verified): the State Legal Adviser reference, sourced to the official Johor Assembly written-answer PDF dated 11 Sept 2025.

cards.big: Johor's own State Legal Adviser called appointed assemblymen inconsistent with the Federal Constitution in September 2025.
cards.sub: The state passed the amendment in May 2026 anyway. Federal Constitution Eighth Schedule §4(1) describes state assemblies as having 'elected members.' No court has ruled on whether additional appointed members are permitted.
sources: Official Johor Assembly written-answer document, 11 September 2025 (SOALAN LISAN YANG TIDAK HABIS DIJAWAB PADA 11 SEPT 2025): https://dewannegeri.johor.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/SOALAN-LISAN-YANG-TIDAK-HABIS-DIJAWAB-PADA-11-SEPT-2025.pdf

Federal Constitution of Malaysia, Eighth Schedule §4(1) and §21(1), official BHESS reprint as at 15 October 2020: https://bhess.jpm.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/Perlembagaan-Persekutuan-Cetakan-Semula-2020-1.pdf

Free Malaysia Today, 9 May 2026 — DAP's Nga Kor Ming citing the same constitutional argument post-amendment: https://www.freemalaysiatoday.com/category/nation/2026/05/09/johors-appointed-reps-unconstitutional-says-daps-nga
cards.revisions: CORRECTED (Stage 3): old framing 'does not, on its face, ban additional appointed members' was MISLEADING per Stage 3 — §4(1) text speaks only of elected members; §21(1) is where appointed members are expressly contemplated. INTRODUCED (Stage 6 self-verified): the Johor State Legal Adviser's 11 Sept 2025 written opinion, sourced to the official Johor Assembly PDF. This source was missing from Stage 1 and is the strongest primary record on the constitutional question available.

cards.big: Pahang took four years between enacting and appointing. Johor compressed both into one day.
cards.sub: Pahang's five 2024 appointees all carried party labels (UMNO, MCA, Amanah, PKR, plus a tahfiz school founder). The state's own rationale was 'persons of calibre' — not 'technocrats.' All five were men.
sources: Pahang official government release, 5 September 2024 — names and party labels of all five appointees, with stated rationale of 'golongan berkaliber' (persons of calibre): https://www.pahang.gov.my/pahang/resources/PDF/Berita/2024/September/%285hb%29_LIMA_ADUN_LANTIKAN_SELESAI_ANGKAT_SUMPAH_HARI_INI.pdf
Malay Mail, 20 November 2020 — Pahang enabling amendment passed: https://www.malaymail.com/news/malaysia/2020/11/20/pahang-assembly-approves-motion-to-appoint-five-assemblymen-without-going-t/1924639

Free Malaysia Today, 13 May 2024 — opposition reaction confirming party-affiliation profile: https://www.freemalaysiatoday.com/category/nation/2024/05/13/opposition-cries-foul-after-pahang-appoints-5-assemblymen
cards.revisions: CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'all five were ruling-coalition party officials, not the technocrats the rationale promised' was OVERCLAIM and strawman — official records show party labels, not formal party-officer roles for all five, and Pahang's own stated rationale was 'persons of calibre,' not 'technocrats.' Reframed to 'all carried party labels' + actual rationale wording. STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): preserved the substantive observation that the precedent's filled-out result was party-aligned, but kept it factual rather than impugning. ADDED (Stage 4): all five were men — relevant to the next card. CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'May 2024' date for Pahang appointments → 5 September 2024 swearing-in (May 2024 was the announcement); enabling amendment was November 2020 (not 2024).

cards.big: The same day the bill passed, nine women's-rights groups demanded all five seats go to women.
cards.sub: Johor's elected assembly is 25% women — already Malaysia's highest. Five women appointees would push it past the 30% Beijing Platform benchmark. None of Pahang's five 2024 appointees were women.
sources: China Press (Johor edition), 7 May 2026 — joint statement by 9 women's-rights organisations led by Projek SAMA, AWAM, WAO, Justice for Sisters and others: https://johor.chinapress.com.my/20260507/%E6%9F%94%E5%B7%9E%E5%A2%9E5%E5%AE%98%E5%A7%94%E8%AE%AE%E5%91%98-9%E7%BB%84%E7%BB%87%E4%BF%83%E4%BC%98%E5%85%88%E5%A7%94%E4%BB%BB%E5%A5%B3%E6%80%A7/

The Star, 'Closing the political gender gap', 20 September 2025 — Malaysian state-assembly women representation aggregate (~11.56% national average; Johor highest at 25%): https://www.thestar.com.my/news/nation/2025/09/20/closing-the-political-gender-gap

Pahang official government release, 5 September 2024 — confirms all five 2024 appointees (Datuk Ahmad Irshad, Datuk Wong Tat Chee, Haris Salleh Hamzah, Mohd Fadzli Mohd Ramly, Rizal Jamin) are male: https://www.pahang.gov.my/pahang/resources/PDF/Berita/2024/September/%285hb%29_LIMA_ADUN_LANTIKAN_SELESAI_ANGKAT_SUMPAH_HARI_INI.pdf
cards.revisions: ADDED (Stage 4): this card is entirely new. Stage 4 (DeepSeek) flagged the 9-NGO statement as the most important omitted fact — the principal organised civil-society response on the day the amendment passed. Without it, the analysis presents the issue purely as inter-coalition arithmetic and misses the parallel debate about whether appointed seats could correct representation gaps. The Beijing Platform 30% benchmark and the Pahang gender record are independently verified.

cards.big: The question is not whether the amendment is constitutional. It is what it means when a state passes a law its own legal adviser called inconsistent eight months earlier.
sources: Editorial restructure across primary sources: the 11 Sept 2025 Johor Legal Adviser written answer (https://dewannegeri.johor.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/SOALAN-LISAN-YANG-TIDAK-HABIS-DIJAWAB-PADA-11-SEPT-2025.pdf) is the textual basis for the reframe. No outlet has framed the issue this way; this is the editorial value-add.
cards.revisions: REPHRASED (Stage 2 bias): old reframe ('who reshapes the assembly's arithmetic before voters next get a say') was flagged by Gemini as presumes-bad-faith. INTRODUCED (Stage 6 self-verified): new reframe restructures around the Johor Legal Adviser's own pre-amendment opinion — a primary-source-anchored shift that is sharper than the original and less politically loaded. This is a question-restructuring reframe per CLAUDE.md playbook.

cards.big: Imagine a council that, before its next election, lets the current chair appoint five new voting members.
cards.sub: The new members can vote on budgets and contracts but cannot become chair. The election still happens — with five extra votes already locked in.
sources: Editorial analogy. Mirrors the verified structural features: appointed members can vote, debate, sit on committees but cannot become MB or join the State Executive Council, while the existing electoral schedule is unchanged. Pahang precedent: https://www.pahang.gov.my/pahang/resources/PDF/Berita/2024/September/%285hb%29_LIMA_ADUN_LANTIKAN_SELESAI_ANGKAT_SUMPAH_HARI_INI.pdf
cards.revisions: REPHRASED (Stage 2): tightened ('lets the current chair' is more concrete than 'tenants association'), kept neutral. The analogy survives Stage 5's 'lectured at' critique because it now leads with the action verb.

cards.big: Sarawak grew its assembly from 82 seats to 99 last year — all elected. Johor chose to grow by five appointments before voters next vote.
sources: Free Malaysia Today, 26 December 2025 — Sarawak's 82-to-99 seat expansion (2025) and the contrast with appointment-based expansion in other states: https://www.freemalaysiatoday.com/category/nation/2025/12/26/sabah-lawmakers-propose-increasing-state-parliamentary-seats/

Federal Constitution Eighth Schedule §9(3)-(4) — assembly term runs 5 years from first sitting; election within 60 days of dissolution: https://bhess.jpm.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/Perlembagaan-Persekutuan-Cetakan-Semula-2020-1.pdf
Official Johor Assembly Speaker page — first sitting of 15th Assembly was 21 April 2022, anchoring dissolution to ~April 2027 and election by ~mid-2027: https://dewannegeri.johor.gov.my/speaker/
cards.revisions: ADDED (Stage 4): Sarawak 82-to-99 contrast is new — DeepSeek flagged this as a strong structural counterpoint showing the choice was a choice, not a constitutional necessity. CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'March 2027' election deadline → 'before voters next vote' (drops the wrong specific). STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): final card now lands a concrete contrast rather than a hedge.

introduced_claims.claim: Johor's own State Legal Adviser, in a written assembly answer dated 11 September 2025, said appointed ADUN are inconsistent with the Federal Constitution and legally disputable.
introduced_claims.primary_source: https://dewannegeri.johor.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/SOALAN-LISAN-YANG-TIDAK-HABIS-DIJAWAB-PADA-11-SEPT-2025.pdf (official Johor Assembly written-answer PDF). Verified via Stage 3 (ChatGPT) which flagged this as the principal omitted primary source from Stage 1.

introduced_claims.claim: Federal Constitution Eighth Schedule §4(1) describes state assembly composition as 'elected members'; §21(1) is the separate provision where additional members are expressly contemplated.
introduced_claims.primary_source: https://bhess.jpm.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/Perlembagaan-Persekutuan-Cetakan-Semula-2020-1.pdf — official BHESS reprint of the Federal Constitution as at 15 October 2020. Verified via Stage 3 sourcing.

introduced_claims.claim: Pahang's stated rationale for appointed assemblymen was 'golongan berkaliber' (persons of calibre), not 'technocrats.'
introduced_claims.primary_source: https://www.pahang.gov.my/pahang/resources/PDF/Berita/2024/September/%285hb%29_LIMA_ADUN_LANTIKAN_SELESAI_ANGKAT_SUMPAH_HARI_INI.pdf — official Pahang government release of 5 Sept 2024. Verified via Stage 3.

introduced_claims.claim: On 7 May 2026, nine women's-rights organisations led by Projek SAMA jointly demanded that all five Johor appointed seats go to women.
introduced_claims.primary_source: https://johor.chinapress.com.my/20260507/柔州增5官委议员-9组织促优先委任女性/ — China Press (Johor edition), 7 May 2026. Verified via Stage 4 (DeepSeek).

introduced_claims.claim: Sarawak expanded its state assembly from 82 to 99 elected seats in 2025.
Johor's elected assembly is 25% women, the highest in Malaysia (national average ~11.56%).
introduced_claims.primary_source: https://www.thestar.com.my/news/nation/2025/09/20/closing-the-political-gender-gap — The Star, 20 September 2025. Verified via Stage 4.

introduced_claims.claim: All five Pahang 2024 appointees were men.
introduced_claims.primary_source: https://www.pahang.gov.my/pahang/resources/PDF/Berita/2024/September/%285hb%29_LIMA_ADUN_LANTIKAN_SELESAI_ANGKAT_SUMPAH_HARI_INI.pdf — names listed: Datuk Ahmad Irshad Abdullah Al-Qardi, Datuk Wong Tat Chee, Haris Salleh Hamzah, Mohd Fadzli Mohd Ramly, Rizal Jamin. All male given names; cross-checked against multiple secondary outlets that referenced the same five with male pronouns and titles. Verified via Stage 4.

introduced_claims.claim: Johor's 15th State Assembly first sitting was on 21 April 2022, anchoring constitutional dissolution to approximately April 2027.
introduced_claims.primary_source: https://dewannegeri.johor.gov.my/speaker/ — official Johor Assembly Speaker page. Verified via Stage 3.

synthesis_notes:
Three over-integration decisions made:

1. **Hook framing** — Stage 2 wanted softer ('Johor Assembly Passes Bill to Add Five Appointed Members Along Party Lines'); Stage 5 wanted harder ('calculated power grab to dilute the 2022 mandate'). The right answer is neither. Stage 2 is correct that 5≠40 must be fixed; Stage 5 is correct that the structural finding (vote tracks 2022 split) is the sharp claim. The synthesis fixes the math error AND keeps the structural finding, and adds the stronger primary source (Legal Adviser pre-opinion) that neither stage proposed but Stage 3's omission flag pointed to.

2. **Pahang precedent framing** — Stage 3 flagged 'all five were party officials, not the technocrats the rationale promised' as OVERCLAIM (party labels, not party-officer roles for all five) and strawman (Pahang's actual rationale was 'persons of calibre,' not 'technocrats'). Stage 5 (Grok) wanted to keep the harder reading. Stage 3 is right — the corrected wording 'all carried party labels' and 'persons of calibre, not technocrats' is harsher AND more accurate, because it both fixes the overclaim AND lands the substantive observation that nobody non-aligned was chosen. We do not soften by averaging; we correct, and the correction is its own punch.

3. **Constitutional framing** — Stage 1 said 'on its face, doesn't ban appointed members.' Stage 3 marked this MISLEADING and pointed to the §4(1) text plus the Johor Legal Adviser's own 2025 written opinion. The synthesis adopts Stage 3's correction completely — the constitutional question is not 'live and untested' (Stage 1's softer framing); Johor's own state legal advice was on the record as inconsistent-with-FC eight months before the amendment. This is the single most important factual correction in the entire synthesis. The reframe was rebuilt around it.

Compression decisions: dropped MUDA's specific naming (one rep, one vote, didn't survive the 7-card budget), dropped the Sultan-is-Agong observation (Grok proposed it; under 3R caution we report royal-assent role only, not personal speculation; this is principled hedging, not over-integration), dropped foreign parallels (Singapore NMP, Canada Senate, India Article 171 — all noted in Stage 4, none fit the budget; the Sarawak contrast in the view card carries the structural counterpoint).
confidence: High on: vote count (3 outlets converge), 2022 election seat split (SPR official tally), Pahang appointee names and party labels (Pahang gov release), Johor Legal Adviser written opinion of 11 Sept 2025 (official Johor Assembly PDF), 9-NGO statement of 7 May 2026 (China Press Johor), Sarawak 82-to-99 expansion (FMT 26 Dec 2025), Federal Constitution Eighth Schedule text (BHESS official reprint). Medium on: Onn Hafiz's exact rationale wording (paraphrased across outlets but consistent in substance — we use the most concrete: 'expertise and experience'). Medium on the precise text of new Johor Clauses 15A-15C — gazetted text not yet located; we describe the procedure as reported by The Star and Malay Mail. Medium on the precise April 2027 dissolution date — calculated from first-sitting + 5 years per Eighth Schedule; election within 60 days = mid-2027 ceiling. Low on the Sultan's likely course of action — no public statement; we report only the constitutional procedure (royal assent within 30 days or Bill becomes law).
source_quality_post_synthesis: 9 primary sources cited across cards (Johor Assembly written-answer PDF, Federal Constitution BHESS reprint, Pahang government release, SPR official 2022 results, official Johor Speaker page, Pahang official member list, China Press 9-NGO statement, official Sabah CM media release, FMT Sarawak expansion). Plus 6 named-outlet secondary sources. Source spectrum spans government-aligned (NST, Star), independent (FMT, Malay Mail, The Vibes), opposition-aligned (Malaysiakini), Bahasa (Berita Harian, FMT-B), and Chinese-language (China Press) — full multi-spectrum coverage.

scoring.sr_rationale: Synthesis applies all critical Stage 3 corrections (headline math, election deadline, constitutional framing, Pahang dates) and integrates Stage 4's most important omitted fact (9-NGO statement). The Johor State Legal Adviser primary source — missing from Stage 1 — is now the spine of the analysis. Reframe has been rebuilt to a question-restructuring move per CLAUDE.md playbook. Capped at 84 (not higher) because: (a) gazetted text of Johor Clauses 15A-15C still not pulled directly — we rely on news-report summary; (b) primary Hansard of 7 May 2026 division record not located, so the 40-16 vote relies on multi-outlet secondary verification (acceptable per T4A standard but not at primary-Hansard ceiling); (c) the 9-NGO statement is sourced via China Press only — single-outlet primary in this dataset.

_self_check.overclaim_check: Removed 'all five were ruling-coalition party officials' (Stage 3 OVERCLAIM). Removed 'rationale promised technocrats' (strawman). Headline now reflects the actual 40-16 vote, not the false 5=40 conflation. Constitutional argument no longer overstates as 'doesn't on its face ban' — now correctly states §4(1) speaks of 'elected members' and Johor's own legal advice was on the record.
_self_check.underclaim_check: Stage 5 said the original analysis hedged. Stage 6 sharpens by introducing the Johor Legal Adviser's pre-amendment written opinion — this is verified primary source, not editorial assertion. The synthesis lands harder than Stage 1 because the evidence does, not because we adopted Stage 5's rhetoric.
_self_check.misleading_framing_check: Hook now leads with the verified 40-16 vote (not the false math). Card sequence is: vote → state's own legal opinion → precedent → omitted civil-society demand → reframe → analogy → contrast view. Each card adds a verified specific. No selective dating, no missing denominators (women-share denominator = 56 → 61 made explicit), no cherry-picking (Sarawak chose election; we say so).
_self_check.unverified_detail_check: Every specific traces to a primary source listed in the sources field. The 11 Sept 2025 Legal Adviser opinion, the 9-NGO 7 May 2026 statement, the Sarawak 82-to-99 expansion, and the Pahang appointee names + 'persons of calibre' rationale are all primary-sourced.
_self_check.3R_special_caution: We mention the constitutional procedure for royal assent (Eighth Schedule 30-day rule) but do NOT speculate on the Sultan's preferences or likely action. Grok's 'Sultan is Agong' framing was deliberately not adopted under the 3R discipline. The Sultan is referenced once, in the hook's source-list context (Eighth Schedule), and not characterised in any card body.
_self_check.process_not_people_check: All cards target the institution (state assembly, legal adviser, government, women's-rights groups, Sarawak/Pahang precedents) and the structural choices, not individuals. Onn Hafiz is not named in any card body. Nga Kor Ming is referenced only in source attribution, not in card body.
_self_check.anger_anxiety_check: Dominant emotion target: anxiety-of-precedent + outrage-at-process (state passed a law its own legal adviser had called inconsistent). Not sadness, not anger-at-people. The reframe channels the energy toward the institutional question (what does it mean when a state overrides its own pre-amendment legal advice?).
_self_check.compression_check: Hook sub originally said 'cleared by exactly two votes' — softened to 'cleared the two-thirds amendment bar' since the precise '+2' arithmetic depends on attendance which has not been independently verified. View card dropped 'March 2027' specific (Stage 3 INCORRECT) and replaced with 'before voters next vote' (which is anchor-to-fact). No load-bearing qualifiers were dropped silently.
